content.text: What it is

Integration tests and end-to-end (E2E) tests are frequently confused in agentic AI projects because both involve running the agent against a real LLM. The distinction is one of scope: an integration test makes real calls to the LLM but stubs all external systems the agent interacts with (databases, third-party APIs, email servers), while an E2E test connects to all real dependencies including external APIs and validates the behavior of the complete user-facing system. For AI workflows, the integration test boundary typically sits between the agent runtime and everything except the LLM; the E2E boundary encompasses the entire stack from the user interface to every external dependency.
Why it matters

Conflating integration and E2E tests leads to test suites that are simultaneously too slow (integration tests run as if they were E2E) and too shallow (E2E tests written at the integration level miss cross-system failures). In agentic AI systems, getting this distinction right is particularly important because the LLM itself introduces non-determinism and cost that must be managed differently at each boundary. A well-defined integration/E2E distinction also clarifies who owns each test type: the engineering team owns integration tests, while QA owns E2E tests.
Benefits
Integration tests catch API contract failures and agent logic bugs without the cost and slowness of full E2E runs.
E2E tests validate real user journeys including UI rendering, authentication, and multi-system state consistency.
Clear scope definitions prevent the expensive anti-pattern of running full E2E tests in per-PR CI pipelines.
Separate test suites allow different failure responses: integration failures block PRs; E2E failures block releases.
Debugging is faster when you know whether a failure is in the LLM integration or in the full system stack.
Trade-offs and limitations
VCR cassettes for integration tests become stale when prompts or model versions change, requiring periodic re-recording.
E2E tests against external APIs are inherently flaky due to network variability and third-party service outages.
The boundary definition requires agreement across teams — disagreement leads to duplicated coverage or critical gaps.
E2E tests in staging require staging versions of every external dependency, which is operationally expensive.
How it works

An integration test for an AI workflow sets up the agent runtime, configures a real LLM client (pointing to the actual API, not a mock), and replaces all external tool dependencies with test doubles. For a customer service agent, this means the LLM call is real but the CRM lookup, email sender, and order database are all returning pre-configured fixture responses. The test runs the agent on a specific input and asserts that the agent called the LLM correctly, used the right tool, and produced the expected response structure.

An E2E test starts a user-facing interaction — a browser action, an API request, a chat message — and follows the full flow through the frontend, backend, agent runtime, LLM API, and every external tool. It asserts on user-visible outcomes: the response shown in the UI, the email received in an inbox, the database record updated. It does not inspect internal agent state or individual LLM call content.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Diagram comparing scope of integration tests versus end-to-end tests in AI workflows

content.text: Integration tests exercise the boundary between the agent runtime and the LLM. E2E tests exercise every boundary simultaneously. The key diagnostic question for placing a test is: how many real external connections does this test make?
When to use it

Use integration tests for: validating that the agent sends the correct prompt to the LLM for a given input, verifying tool selection routing, confirming that retry logic handles specific error responses correctly, and checking that output parsing handles the range of real model response formats. Use E2E tests for: validating that a user query produces the correct visible output, confirming that a tool action actually updates an external system, and verifying that the full authentication and authorization flow works for the agent's API endpoints.
How to implement
Define the integration boundary in a team glossary: integration tests may call the real LLM and stub everything else.
Use pytest-vcr or a dedicated LLM caching layer (like LangChain's SQLite cache) to record and replay LLM responses in integration tests for deterministic re-runs.
Create test doubles for every external tool: a FakeCRMClient, FakeEmailClient, FakeOrderDatabase that return controlled fixture data.
Mark integration tests with @pytest.mark.integration and run them in a CI job that runs after every merge, not on every PR.
For E2E tests, provision a staging environment with real (sandboxed) versions of external APIs; use Playwright for UI-based journeys.
Set a hard rule: E2E tests run only in the staging environment on release branches, never in the unit/integration CI job.
Examples

# Integration test: real LLM, all tools stubbed
import pytest
from unittest.mock import MagicMock
from agent.core import SupportAgent

@pytest.fixture
def stubbed_tools():
    crm = MagicMock()
    crm.lookup_customer.return_value = {"name": "Alice", "tier": "gold", "orders": 5}
    email = MagicMock()
    return {"crm": crm, "email": email}

@pytest.mark.integration
def test_agent_escalates_angry_customer(stubbed_tools, vcr_cassette):
    """Real LLM decides to escalate; tool is stubbed."""
    with vcr_cassette("angry_escalation.yaml"):
        agent = SupportAgent(tools=stubbed_tools)
        result = agent.handle("I've been waiting for 3 weeks and nobody helps me!")
    # Real LLM will decide to escalate — cassette captures that decision
    assert stubbed_tools["email"].send_escalation.called
    assert result.resolved is False

# E2E test: full system in staging
@pytest.mark.e2e
def test_user_receives_escalation_email(staging_browser, staging_inbox):
    """User submits complaint; real email is delivered."""
    staging_browser.goto("https://staging.support.example.com")
    staging_browser.fill("#query", "I've been waiting for 3 weeks and nobody helps me!")
    staging_browser.click("#submit")
    staging_browser.wait_for_selector(".response-rendered")
    # Poll staging email inbox for up to 30 seconds
    email = staging_inbox.wait_for_email(subject="Your case has been escalated", timeout=30)
    assert email is not None
    assert "3 weeks" in email.body
Real-world example

A fintech startup building a loan status agent had 400 integration tests covering every error case their LLM router handled: invalid loan IDs, rate limits, partial CRM data, model refusals. These ran in 2 minutes on every PR merge. They had 8 E2E tests that ran on staging weekly, covering the most critical user journeys: new loan status check, refinance inquiry, payment due reminder. When the CRM API changed its response schema, the integration tests caught it in 2 hours (post-merge CI). When a frontend rendering bug hid the escalation button, only the E2E tests caught it — the integration tests could not see the UI.
Common mistakes
Making real external API calls in integration tests, turning them into expensive and flaky E2E tests masquerading as integration tests.
Not recording LLM cassettes: running live LLM calls in integration tests makes them non-deterministic and expensive.
Writing E2E tests that assert on internal agent state instead of external outcomes — these break with any internal refactor.
Running E2E tests in the per-PR CI pipeline where they block developers for 20+ minutes on unrelated failures.
Having no clear owner for E2E test maintenance: flaky E2E tests that are not fixed become ignored and eventually deleted.
Best practices
Document the integration/E2E boundary in TESTING.md so every engineer knows which test type to write for a given scenario.
Record VCR cassettes using a low-cost model (Haiku instead of Sonnet) to minimize the cost of re-recording after prompt changes.
Run integration tests post-merge in a fast CI job; run E2E tests on a daily schedule and on release branches only.
For each E2E test, document what it covers, why it must be E2E (not integration), and the last date its cassettes or fixtures were updated.
Treat E2E test flakiness as a blocking bug — a flaky E2E test provides no signal and should be fixed or deleted within one sprint.
Performance and scalability

Integration test suites for AI agents should target 3 minutes or fewer for 100 tests, achieved by parallelizing tests across cores (pytest-xdist) and using cached VCR cassettes rather than live LLM calls. E2E tests scale poorly by design — each test touches multiple external systems. Cap E2E suites at 15 tests maximum; any scenario beyond that count should be validated by integration tests instead. If the E2E suite exceeds 15 minutes total run time, it will be skipped under deadline pressure, defeating its purpose.

Security and reliability

E2E tests that run against staging environments must use sandboxed credentials with minimal privileges: read-only access to databases, sandbox-mode for payment APIs, disposable email inboxes. Never use production credentials in E2E tests. Integration test cassettes may capture LLM response content that includes test user data; store cassettes in version-controlled repositories with appropriate access restrictions and periodically audit them for inadvertent PII capture.
Deciding the boundary: a decision framework
When a new test scenario arises, use this four-question framework to decide whether to write an integration test or an E2E test:
Does the test need to verify a user-visible outcome in the frontend UI? → E2E only.
Does the test require a real external system to change state (email sent, database record created, payment processed)? → E2E.
Does the test primarily validate agent logic, prompt behavior, or tool routing? → Integration.
Does the test need to run on every pull request without significant time or cost? → Integration.

In practice, most test scenarios belong in the integration layer. E2E tests should be reserved for scenarios where the boundary between systems is the thing being validated, not the agent's internal behavior. A useful heuristic: if a product manager or QA engineer would write the test, it is E2E. If a software engineer writing the agent would write it, it is integration.
Classifying example scenarios
Scenario
Integration or E2E?
Why
Agent selects web_search tool for factual question
Integration
Internal routing logic, tool call is stubbed
User sees correct answer in chat UI
E2E
Validates UI rendering of agent output
Agent retries after 429 rate limit
Integration
Retry logic with real LLM mock response, no UI
Escalation email arrives in customer inbox
E2E
Validates real email delivery to external inbox
Agent output passes safety scoring threshold
Eval (neither)
Quality scoring belongs in the eval layer
Managing VCR cassette lifecycle

VCR cassettes for integration tests have their own lifecycle. When a prompt template changes significantly, the cassette recording a response to that prompt may no longer be valid — the model would respond differently to the updated prompt. Teams need a process for keeping cassettes current:

Tag each cassette with the prompt version hash (a short hash of the system prompt) in its filename. When the prompt hash changes, tests using cassettes tagged with old hashes are automatically detected as stale.

Run a weekly cassette freshness job in CI that re-records all cassettes tagged as stale using a dedicated low-cost model and commits the updated cassettes to a separate PR for human review before merging.

Keep cassettes in a dedicated directory (tests/cassettes/) with a .gitattributes rule marking them as binary or large-file-storage objects to prevent diff noise in PRs that touch both code and cassettes.

Review cassette diffs carefully: a change in a cassette is a change in what the model outputs for that input, which may indicate a model behavior change worth investigating even if the test still passes.

# cassette naming convention with prompt hash
tests/cassettes/
  order_lookup__prompt_a3f9b2.yaml    # hash of system prompt version
  angry_escalation__prompt_a3f9b2.yaml
  order_lookup__prompt_c7d1e4.yaml    # updated cassette after prompt change

# CI check: fail if any cassette hash does not match current prompt hash
python3 ci/check_cassette_freshness.py --cassette-dir tests/cassettes/ --prompt-dir src/prompts/
Setting up an E2E test environment for AI workflows

E2E tests for agentic AI require a staging environment that mirrors production but replaces external services with sandboxed equivalents that are safe to trigger in a test context. The key infrastructure components for a robust AI E2E environment are:

Staging LLM endpoint: either the same production LLM API with a staging API key (for cost tracking) or a dedicated test deployment of an open-source model. Using the production API in staging with a budget cap (e.g., $50/month) is simpler and more representative than running a separate model.

Sandboxed external APIs: for email delivery, use Mailhog or Mailtrap in sandbox mode. For payment APIs, use Stripe test mode. For CRM systems, use a seeded staging database instance. Never use production credentials.

Test user accounts: pre-created user accounts with known data in the staging database so tests do not depend on dynamic state. Use a test-data seeder script that runs before the E2E suite to reset the staging database to a known state.

Isolated test runs: configure the CI system so only one E2E test suite runs at a time against the staging environment to prevent test interference. Use a distributed lock (e.g., Redis) if parallel E2E runs are needed.
Screenshot and trace capture: configure Playwright to save screenshots and network traces on test failure so engineers can diagnose failures without re-running the test.
Handling LLM non-determinism in tests

A significant challenge for both integration and E2E tests in AI workflows is that LLM responses are non-deterministic: the same prompt may produce slightly different wording on each call. This non-determinism must be managed differently at each test layer:

Integration layer: use VCR cassettes to replay recorded responses, or set temperature=0 and a fixed seed in the LLM call to maximize determinism. Note that temperature=0 does not guarantee identical responses across different model versions.

E2E layer: assert on semantics, not exact text. Rather than asserting 'response == expected_exact_string', use flexible checks: 'expected keyword in response' or a semantic similarity function. For critical E2E assertions, use a lightweight classifier to check that the response falls into the correct category.

Both layers: run the test suite multiple times (3-5 runs) on any test that exhibits intermittent failures before classifying it as flaky. A single failure on a semantics-asserting test may be legitimate LLM variance, not a bug.

# Flexible E2E assertion for non-deterministic LLM output
def assert_response_mentions_escalation(response_text: str):
    """Allow for LLM's natural language variance in escalation responses."""
    escalation_indicators = [
        "escalate", "transfer", "senior", "specialist",
        "human agent", "manager", "priority", "urgent"
    ]
    text_lower = response_text.lower()
    matched = [ind for ind in escalation_indicators if ind in text_lower]
    assert len(matched) >= 2, (
        f"Response should mention escalation. Found only: {matched}\n"        f"Response: {response_text[:200]}"
    )
Key takeaways
Integration tests make real LLM calls but stub all external tools; E2E tests connect to every real dependency.
Run integration tests post-merge in CI; run E2E tests on a schedule and pre-release only.
VCR cassettes make integration tests deterministic and cheap by replaying recorded LLM responses.
E2E tests validate user-visible outcomes, not internal agent state.
Cap E2E test suites at 15 tests; any additional coverage beyond that belongs in integration tests.
Treat E2E flakiness as a blocking bug to maintain suite credibility.
